subquestions.text: Does Okafor have a disqualifying financial interest in a Harbor Light contract under Government Code section 1090, arising either from (a) the spouse's $95,000 salaried position (partially funded by city/HHAP money) or (b) Okafor's own uncompensated board seat, or both independently?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the threshold question. If neither the spouse's salary nor the uncompensated directorship counts as a section 1090 financial interest, the entire analysis collapses and no remote-interest exception, recusal, or resignation question ever needs to be reached. Section 1090 case law (e.g., Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor Agency v. Del Norte County) treats indirect and non-monetary interests broadly, and spousal income is often imputed, but whether an uncompensated board seat alone (without personal financial benefit) counts as a section 1090 interest is a distinct sub-issue from the spousal-salary theory.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Research would need to confirm (i) whether case law/AG opinions treat a spouse's employment income as automatically creating a section 1090 interest for the official (independent of community-property PRA analysis), (ii) whether 'financial interest' under section 1090 requires potential personal pecuniary gain/loss and whether an uncompensated directorship satisfies that absent salary, and (iii) whether the city-contract share of Harbor Light's operating budget (40%) is sufficiently direct/foreseeable to make the spouse's job (and thus Okafor's interest) 'in' this specific RFP contract versus too attenuated.

key_considerations: Section 1090's 'financially interested' standard is construed broadly and prophylactically (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633; Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), and courts have imputed a spouse's financial stake to the official via community-property/quasi-community-property principles (Fam. Code § 760) even outside the PRA's explicit spousal-imputation text. People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 appears closely on-point for spousal ties to a nonprofit receiving public funds and, if its facts/holding verify as recalled, supports finding a disqualifying interest independent of PRA § 87103 — this is the strongest single authority pushing the estimate upward, but its exact holding and procedural posture (statute-of-limitations reversal) need confirmation.

Government Code § 1091(b) contains an enumerated 'remote interest' category for noncompensated officers/directors of nonprofit corporations (verify exact subsection, plausibly (b)(8)), meaning the board seat alone is likely a curable remote interest via disclosure/abstention/noninvolvement rather than a fatal, non-curable interest by itself — this cuts against treating prong (b) as automatically voiding the contract, even though Okafor still could not vote on it.

The spouse's $95k salary is not obviously covered by any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest category for spousal employment by a contracting nonprofit (no confirmed carve-out found), so if it counts as an 'interest' at all it likely falls outside the statutory cure mechanisms — making prong (a) the more legally serious basis and the one doing most of the work in a 'yes' conclusion, and potentially tainting reliance on the remote-interest cure for prong (b) as well under a totality-of-interests approach.

Attenuation is the real fight on prong (a): funds flow into Harbor Light's general operating budget (40% city-derived) rather than being earmarked to the spouse's salary line, and this specific $2.3M RFP is only part of that 40% stream. But the spouse's title — Director of Programs — suggests she likely administers the very programs this RFP would fund, which strengthens directness/foreseeability and cuts against an attenuation defense; AG opinions on how direct a funding nexus must be to trigger § 1090 need to be located and checked against this fact pattern.

Section 1090's threshold inquiry (is there a 'financial interest' at all) is analytically prior to and distinct from the § 1091/1091.5 exception analysis and from PRA materiality/public-generally analysis; this sub-question isolates only the threshold interest question, so the estimate should not be discounted for cure/remedy availability, only for whether an interest exists in the first place — which the breadth of 1090 case law and the Honig-type spousal-nonprofit fact pattern make more likely than not to exist for at least the spousal-salary prong.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Confirming the actual holding/facts of People v. Honig on the spousal-nonprofit-employment point would be decisive either way. Locating any AG opinion or case squarely addressing spousal salary from a nonprofit whose revenue is only partially (not directly/earmarked) derived from the specific contract at issue — and how courts draw the directness/foreseeability line — would materially move the estimate. Confirming the precise text of Government Code § 1091(b) and § 1091.5(a) to see whether any subsection covers spousal employment by a nonprofit contracting party (as opposed to just noncompensated director status) would also be decisive: if such a carve-out exists and applies, the probability should drop; if confirmed absent, it should rise further.

subquestions.text: If Okafor has a section 1090 financial interest, does any statutory carve-out apply - specifically, is the uncompensated nonprofit-board seat a remote interest under section 1091(b) (and if so which subsection, e.g., a noncompensated-officer/director-of-nonprofit category) or a noninterest under section 1091.5, or does any provision address a spouse's employment by the contracting entity as a noninterest - and what disclosure/abstention procedure would be required to preserve the contract's validity if an exception applies?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Sections 1091 and 1091.5 are the only statutory escape valves from an otherwise absolute section 1090 bar. Whether Okafor's specific facts fit within a listed remote-interest or noninterest category, rather than a personal financial interest outside any exception, determines whether disclosure-plus-abstention can ever validate the contract, or whether section 1090's flat prohibition controls regardless of recusal.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need pinpoint verification of the current text of section 1091(b) and section 1091.5(a) to identify any subsection covering (i) uncompensated nonprofit directors/officers, (ii) government-designated liaisons to outside boards, or (iii) a spouse's employment relationship, and confirmation via AG opinions or case law of how those categories have been applied to facts resembling council-liaison board seats plus spousal employment.

key_considerations: Government Code § 1091(b) likely does contain a subsection (added to address exactly the 'council-designated liaison to an outside nonprofit board' scenario) exempting a noncompensated officer/director of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation that supports the body's functions or to which the body has an appointment/liaison relationship — this appears squarely aimed at the board-seat piece of Okafor's interest, but the precise subsection number and current wording need pinpoint statutory verification (it has been renumbered/amended over time).

Even if the board-seat interest is a 'remote interest' curable under § 1091(a) (disclosure in the official record + Okafor's total non-participation, including no influence/advocacy behind the scenes, with the remaining members acting without counting him toward quorum), that cure only reaches the board-membership interest — it does not automatically reach the analytically separate financial interest arising from the spouse's $95K salary at Harbor Light, which is independently imputed to Okafor under § 1090/community-property principles.

I have low confidence that any § 1091(b) remote-interest or § 1091.5(a) noninterest category specifically covers a spouse's compensated employment with a contracting nonprofit whose revenue is materially dependent (~40%) on the very contracts being awarded — the closer statutory analogues (e.g., rank-and-file employee whose duties don't touch the contract, or fixed-salary employee unaffected by contract outcome) generally require that the position not be affected by the award, which is doubtful here given Harbor Light's budget dependency and the spouse's management-level 'Director of Programs' title.

Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and related authority hold that mere recusal by a sitting board member generally does NOT cure a § 1090 conflict (unlike under the Political Reform Act) absent a statutory remote-interest/noninterest exception with its specific procedural predicate satisfied — so if the spousal-employment interest doesn't independently fall into an enumerated exception, disclosure-and-abstention alone likely cannot save the contract as to that interest, regardless of how the board-seat interest is resolved.

Because two distinct financial interests are in play (board seat and spousal salary), a full 'carve-out cures everything' outcome requires BOTH to independently qualify for an exception with its procedure properly followed; the compound probability of that being true is materially lower than the probability that the board-seat piece alone qualifies.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Pinpoint text-check of the current (2026) Government Code §§ 1091(b) and 1091.5(a) subsections and their legislative history/AG interpretive opinions specifically addressing (1) noncompensated nonprofit officer/director liaison seats and (2) a public official's spouse employed by a contracting nonprofit whose funding depends materially on the contract at issue; also any FPPC or AG opinions or published case law applying these provisions to facts combining both a governance seat and a spouse's compensation tied to the contracting entity's budget, and confirmation of whether the spouse's compensation is fixed/unaffected by the specific award (which would support a noninterest finding) versus variable or job-security-linked (which would defeat it).

subquestions.text: For a councilmember who is a sitting member of the very body awarding the contract, does recusal/abstention from the vote ever cure a section 1090 violation (as opposed to merely satisfying Political Reform Act disclosure/recusal rules), or does controlling case law treat the contract as void or voidable regardless of the interested member's non-participation because the interest of one member infects the whole governing body's action?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is likely the single biggest crux driving the city attorney's advice: PRA compliance is typically satisfied by recusal, but section 1090 has long been read (Thomson v. Call, Stigall v. City of Taft, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor) as forbidding the body itself from making the contract when one of its own members has a financial interest, subject only to the statutory remote-interest/noninterest exceptions, meaning recusal alone may not save the contract at all.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Requires confirming whether the current case law's contamination rule applies at the level of the full council (as opposed to a subordinate board/commission separate from the contracting body), whether any judicially or AG-recognized segmentation or independent-panel approach has been accepted as curing section 1090 for a full-body vote, and whether the rule of necessity (allowing an interested member to vote only when no quorum otherwise exists) is even in play here given Rio Robles presumably has enough non-interested councilmembers.

key_considerations: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 is the controlling case: it held a contract void under §1090 even though the interested councilmember abstained from the final vote, because he participated in earlier negotiations/discussions as a body member — the court reasoned §1090 targets the official's position to influence, not just the recorded vote, so 'mere' vote-abstention by a sitting member of the contracting body generally does NOT cure the violation.

People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 and related authority read 'making' a contract broadly (participating in developing specs/criteria, negotiations, agenda influence, even informal advocacy) — here, the RFP-framework vote (step (a)) is itself likely 'making' activity, so recusing only from the later award vote (step (b)) probably would not insulate the framework decision or the overall contract from taint if Okafor was involved earlier.

The recognized escape valves are narrow and statutory, not a freestanding 'recusal cures it' doctrine: (1) a §1091 remote-interest category (with disclosure + total non-participation in discussion AND vote) or (2) a §1091.5 noninterest category — if the spouse's employment/board seat fits one of these enumerated categories, the specific disclosure-and-abstain procedure built into that statute (not generic recusal) can validate the contract; if it fits neither, contamination controls.

Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 'rule of necessity' is the other narrow exception, but per the sub-question's own framing Rio Robles presumably has a quorum of non-interested councilmembers, so necessity is almost certainly unavailable here, reinforcing that the general contamination rule (not an exception) governs.

AG opinions (recurring theme across multiple opinions on spouse-employment-at-contracting-nonprofit fact patterns) consistently counsel that for a full governing-body member, recusal alone is treated as insufficient and that resignation from the conflicted position or structural removal of the interest is the only administratively safe course — courts/AG are notably more skeptical of 'segmented' or 'independent panel' workarounds when the interested official remains a sitting member of the body with ultimate approval authority over the same matter.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Evidence that would lower this probability: (1) confirmation that Okafor's specific interest (spouse's salaried nonprofit employment + uncompensated board seat) squarely fits an enumerated §1091 remote-interest or §1091.5 noninterest category, making the statutory disclosure-and-abstain mechanism (rather than generic recusal) formally available and sufficient; (2) case law or a later Court of Appeal/Supreme Court decision narrowing or distinguishing Thomson v. Call for full-council (as opposed to smaller/special-purpose board) contexts; (3) evidence the council could lawfully and completely delegate final contracting authority to a genuinely independent body (e.g., a charter provision or ordinance vesting award authority in a staff officer or separate commission with no council ratification step), which some AG guidance treats differently from mere abstention by a sitting member; (4) confirmation that Rio Robles actually lacks a non-interested quorum, which would put the rule-of-necessity exception in play and change the analysis substantially.

subquestions.text: Does Okafor's participation in adopting the RFP framework and scoring criteria (step a) itself constitute prohibited making of the contract under section 1090's broad definition (covering preliminary discussions, negotiations, drafting terms, and influence over specifications), such that recusing only from the later award vote (step b) fails to cure the taint even if step (b) recusal would otherwise suffice?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Section 1090 case law defines making a contract expansively to include involvement in planning, negotiating, and setting terms, not just the final vote. If setting the scoring criteria that will determine which bidder (likely Harbor Light) wins counts as making, then recusing only from the award vote does not solve the problem, and Okafor would need to be walled off from the framework-setting stage as well.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need case-specific confirmation (e.g., Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, People v. Honig, Stigall) that criteria-setting/preliminary-stage participation has been held sufficient to constitute making a contract for section 1090 purposes, and whether that taint can be segregated from a later, properly recused award vote or instead retroactively voids the whole process.

key_considerations: California courts define '[making]' under §1090 very broadly — Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 and People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 both hold that 'making' reaches the planning, preliminary discussions, negotiations, and drafting of specifications/terms, not just the final approving vote; Honig specifically rejects the idea that abstaining at the final stage cures earlier substantive involvement.

Setting RFP scoring criteria is arguably the single most outcome-determinative act in a competitive procurement — it is analogous to the 'drawing of plans and specifications' language courts have treated as core 'making' conduct, and Harbor Light's historical ~60% win rate strengthens the inference that criteria design can channel the award, which is the harm §1090 targets regardless of proven intent.

Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222 support the proposition that recusal from a later vote does not retroactively purge taint from earlier participation in the same contract-formation process — but neither is a squarely identical RFP-criteria fact pattern, so extension to this exact scenario is an inference, not a holding on point.

Countervailing argument (the main source of residual uncertainty): the RFP framework/criteria could be characterized as a general, facially neutral legislative/policy act applicable to all potential bidders rather than a step 'in' the Harbor Light contract specifically; some §1090 authority and Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050 discuss segmenting individual member conduct within multi-member bodies, which could support a narrower reading if Okafor's role in criteria-setting was truly generic and not shown to have been shaped with Harbor Light in mind — this is the strongest counter and keeps the estimate below ~85%.

No case was identified (and none should be assumed) squarely holding that RFP-criteria adoption specifically, as opposed to sole-source negotiation or direct deal-shaping, satisfies §1090's 'making' element — the probability reflects strong doctrinal analogy plus prosecutorial/FPPC risk-aversion in this area rather than a confirmed on-point holding.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: A published California case (or CA AG/FPPC opinion) squarely addressing whether a public official's vote on generic, bidder-neutral RFP scoring criteria — where the official has no role in drafting, weighting toward, or discussing the criteria with the eventual awardee — has been held NOT to constitute 'making' under §1090 when followed by full recusal from the award vote, would lower this. Conversely, a case directly on point (RFP/grant-criteria stage) applying Honig's reasoning to void the resulting contract despite later recusal would push this toward 90%+. Also relevant: any evidence in the full record about whether Okafor's participation in step (a) included specific advocacy, comments, or influence favoring criteria that predictably favored Harbor Light versus purely ministerial/unanimous-consent participation, since more active involvement strengthens the 'making' finding and passive/minimal involvement weakens it toward the Lexin-style segmentation argument."

subquestions.text: Independent of section 1090, does the Political Reform Act (sections 87100, 87103) disqualify Okafor from both votes based on a community-property interest in the spouse's salary and the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the RFP/award decisions on Harbor Light, or does the public-generally exception (section 87103 and FPPC regulations such as Reg. 18707) apply because the decision affects a broad class of homelessness-service contracts/providers rather than Harbor Light uniquely - and if the Act is satisfied by recusal while section 1090 is not, how do the two regimes interact for advice purposes?
subquestions.why_it_matters: The Act and section 1090 are analytically distinct: the Act generally treats recusal as a full cure, materiality thresholds and the public-generally exception can excuse even a real financial interest, and FPPC guidance/regulations directly address spousal income and community property. But because section 1090 (per q3/q4) may not be cured by recusal, the city attorney needs to know that PRA compliance alone is necessary but insufficient, and why the two statutes can lead to different bottom lines on the same facts.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Requires verifying current FPPC regulatory thresholds for materiality of income to a spouse's employer under Reg. 18702.1/18702.2 (or successor regs), confirming whether the public-generally exception has been applied or rejected on facts where only three realistic bidders exist and one has a historical 60% win rate, suggesting the effect is not shared by a significant segment of the public, and clarifying doctrinally that Act compliance does not immunize a section 1090 violation.

key_considerations: Community-property attribution: under Gov. Code § 82030 and FPPC reg (formerly 18703.5, renumbering effective 2023 — verify current cite), a spouse's salary is treated as income/financial interest of the official, and Okafor's own board-director seat independently triggers § 87103(c)/(d)-type interests — two independent bases for disqualification, making the 'no financial interest at all' argument weak.

The public-generally exception (§ 87103 'distinguishable from the public generally,' operationalized via the FPPC's 'significant segment'/disproportionate-effect test, current Reg. cite needs verification post-2023 renumbering — was 18707 region) is applied narrowly; with only three realistic bidders and a 60% historical win rate for Harbor Light, the affected class is small and non-representative, and effects are not evenly distributed — this fact pattern closely resembles advice letters/opinions where the exception was rejected rather than granted, but no on-point published case with identical facts is being asserted, only pattern-matching to FPPC guidance.

Materiality thresholds (Reg. 18702.1/18702.2 successor provisions) turn on percentage of Harbor Light's revenue affected; the stated fact that city contracts already fund ~40% of Harbor Light's operating budget and this RFP is the primary vehicle for that money strongly supports a finding of material effect on the framework vote too (foreseeability doctrine extends to upstream criteria-setting votes when the bidder pool and historical win pattern are known).

Doctrinal severability between regimes: case law (e.g., Thomson v. Call (1984) 38 Cal.3d 633; People v. Honig (1996); Lexin v. Superior Court (2010)) supports that recusal/non-participation is the FPPC/Act's designed and sufficient remedy under §§ 87100/87105, whereas § 1090 has been construed to void a contract based on the mere existence of a financial interest in a board member regardless of abstention, subject only to the narrow § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest carve-outs — this asymmetry is well-supported but the precise scope of 'remote interest' cure for a spouse's salaried employment (vs. investment) interest needs specific verification against § 1091(b) categories.

Compound-conclusion risk: the sub-question bundles four distinct legal determinations (spousal-income interest exists; public-generally exception fails; recusal cures Act; recusal does not cure § 1090); each individually is well-supported by conventional PRA/§1090 doctrine, but conjunctive certainty is necessarily lower than any single component, and an FPPC advice letter or superseding regulation on these specific facts could alter the public-generally analysis.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: An actual FPPC advice/opinion letter or enforcement decision applying the public-generally exception to a small (2-4 bidder) competitive RFP pool for a public-benefit category of services would most directly move this estimate — if the FPPC has found such narrow bidder pools still qualify for the exception because the *category* of decision (broad service-type criteria) rather than the *bidder* is the relevant unit of analysis, probability should drop significantly. Conversely, a published FPPC opinion squarely rejecting the exception on a near-identical 2-3-bidder/incumbent-dominance fact pattern would push this above 85. On the § 1090/PRA-interaction question, a case squarely holding that adequate recusal by a board member does cure § 1090 (undermining Thomson v. Call's reasoning) would lower confidence in that half of the answer; absence of such authority (as currently believed) supports the estimate. Also relevant: confirming the current (post-2023 renumbering) citations for Regs. 18702.1/18702.2/18707 and verifying they still contain the same material-effect thresholds and public-generally test described.

subquestions.text: What is the realistic menu of structural remedies (spouse's resignation/reassignment away from city-funded duties, Okafor's resignation from the Harbor Light board, rescinding or amending the 2019 liaison resolution, full recusal plus independent scoring panel, contract restructuring to exclude Harbor Light or wall off HHAP funds, or simply not contracting with Harbor Light) that would actually eliminate the section 1090/PRA problems prospectively, and what are the concrete legal consequences (contract voidness under section 1092, disgorgement exposure, misdemeanor criminal liability under section 1097, FPPC administrative penalties, and HHAP state-funding audit/clawback risk) if the city proceeds without adequately curing the conflict?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This converts the doctrinal analysis into the actual advice Okafor and the city attorney need: which combination of steps, if any, is sufficient, which are necessary but not sufficient, and what the city's downside exposure is if it guesses wrong, including whether disgorgement/voidness would fall on Harbor Light (an innocent third party) as well as on Okafor personally, and whether HHAP as state pass-through funding creates additional state-law compliance exposure beyond section 1090/the Act.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need confirmation of section 1092's voidability mechanics and any limitations period, whether disgorgement under section 1090 case law (e.g., Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla, Lexin v. Superior Court) reaches non-official third parties like Harbor Light or only the official's own compensation, the mens rea/willfulness requirement for section 1097 criminal liability, current FPPC penalty ranges and enforcement priorities for spousal-income conflicts, and whether HHAP program rules impose independent conflict-of-interest certification requirements that could trigger state clawback regardless of the section 1090/PRA outcome.

key_considerations: Framing of the estimate: probability that RECUSAL ALONE (Okafor abstaining from discussion/vote on both the RFP framework and the award, even paired with an independent scoring panel and 'walled-off' HHAP funds) is legally SUFFICIENT to cure the Section 1090 problem without also requiring board resignation, rescission of the 2019 liaison resolution, and/or severing the spouse's compensation from city-funded revenue. I estimate this at ~10% (i.e., ~90% likely recusal alone does NOT suffice).

Doctrinal driver: Section 1090's 'non-cure by abstention' rule for a sitting member of the contracting body itself. Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 (verify pinpoint holdings) stand for the proposition that when the interested official sits on the very body approving the contract, mere non-participation in the vote does not remove the taint — the statutory 'remote interest' cure of recusal-plus-disclosure is available only for the specific categories enumerated in §1091, and the 'noninterest' cure only for the categories in §1091.5, not as a general common-law abstention doctrine. Unless Okafor's board-liaison role fits squarely within a §1091/1091.5 category (e.g., possibly §1091.5(a)(8)-type nonprofit-director noninterest — verify exact text and whether spousal compensation independently disqualifies it), recusal from the vote is likely legally inadequate.

Spousal income as an independent, freestanding disqualifying interest: even if Okafor resigned the board seat entirely, the spouse's ~$95k salary from an entity receiving ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts is likely treated as Okafor's own financial interest under §1090 via community-property attribution, and as 'income from a source' under Gov. Code §87103(c) for PRA purposes — meaning board resignation alone, or recusal alone, addresses only one leg of a two-leg conflict (board membership AND spousal employment) and probably leaves the other leg unresolved.

Fungibility problem for 'wall' or 'contract-structuring' remedies: because nonprofit general operating budgets commingle funding sources, segregating HHAP funds from Harbor Light's general operating budget (which pays the spouse) or restricting the award to non-HHAP dollars is unlikely to be recognized by a court as eliminating the financial interest — §1090 case law generally treats the whole contract, not a carved-out funding stream, as the relevant unit of analysis (verify against any case addressing partial/segregated funding as a cure). This makes 'contract restructuring while keeping Harbor Light as awardee' a weak remedy on its own.

Genuine uncertainty / first-impression risk: this exact fact pattern (uncompensated council-liaison board seat at a nonprofit + spouse's salaried job there, funded substantially by city contracts) does not map cleanly onto a single controlling case; the analysis rests on analogizing Honig, Lexin, Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla, and PRA materiality regulations (2 CCR §18702.1 et seq., verify current cite), which increases residual uncertainty in either direction — a well-argued position could exist that materiality/attenuation principles borrowed from the PRA should inform whether the spousal interest is disqualifying at all, though §1090 is traditionally understood as a stricter, non-materiality-based bright-line rule.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would raise the probability that recusal alone suffices: (1) confirmation that Okafor's uncompensated liaison directorship falls within a §1091 remote-interest or §1091.5 noninterest category that expressly contemplates nonprofit-board service by a public official and treats it as curable by disclosure/abstention regardless of a spouse's employment there; (2) case law or an AG opinion holding that a spouse's salary from a 40%-city-funded nonprofit is too attenuated/non-material to constitute a §1090 'financial interest' at all; (3) authority that §1090's 'interested body member' rule applies only to the vote itself and that full withdrawal from all deliberation, drafting, and scoring (not just the vote) satisfies the statute even for a non-remote interest. Would lower it further/confirm: any case squarely holding recusal by an interested sitting member never cures §1090 regardless of exception category, or an FPPC/AG opinion treating spousal nonprofit-executive income funded by the same agency's contracts as a per se disqualifying, non-curable-by-recusal interest.
